topic.what: A multicenter RCT evaluating whether adding polaprezinc to standard clarithromycin-based triple therapy improves H. pylori eradication rates and reduces gastric mucosal inflammation compared with triple therapy alone.
topic.how: Randomised controlled trial across multiple centres; patients with H. pylori-positive gastritis received triple therapy (PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin) with or without adjunct polaprezinc; primary endpoints were eradication rate (urea breath test) and histological inflammation scores.
topic.why_matters: H. pylori eradication rates with standard triple therapy have declined due to antibiotic resistance; adjunctive agents that improve eradication and simultaneously reduce mucosal damage could improve patient outcomes.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Proportion of patients achieving successful H. pylori eradication by urea breath test at 4 weeks post-treatment
characteristics.value: Significantly higher in polaprezinc + triple therapy arm
characteristics.evidence: ITT and PP analyses in multicenter RCT
characteristics.context: Asian patient population; clarithromycin-sensitive strains assumed

characteristics.what_it_measures: Histological degree of neutrophil and mononuclear infiltration in gastric biopsies (Updated Sydney System)
characteristics.value: Reduced inflammation in adjunct polaprezinc group
characteristics.context: Antrum and corpus biopsies; improvement more pronounced in antrum

characteristics.what_it_measures: Incidence of adverse events (GI side effects, nausea, diarrhoea)
characteristics.value: Comparable tolerability; no additional AEs from polaprezinc
characteristics.evidence: Self-reported AE diary and clinician assessment

good_for: Clinical protocols seeking to augment H. pylori eradication rates beyond standard triple therapy
Patients with significant mucosal inflammation requiring concurrent mucosal protection
Populations where clarithromycin-based triple therapy is still first-line
not_for: Clarithromycin-resistant H. pylori strains (evidence base limited)
Quadruple bismuth therapy contexts (different adjunct logic)
Long-term maintenance therapy (trial focused on eradication course)
requires: Clarithromycin susceptibility testing or low local resistance rates
Access to polaprezinc (primarily available in Japan and parts of Asia)

novelty: First multicenter RCT demonstrating that polaprezinc as an adjunct to standard triple therapy improves both eradication rate and mucosal inflammation endpoints
Provides clinical-level evidence connecting the gastroprotective mechanism of zinc-carnosine to meaningful anti-H. pylori outcomes
Supports integrative use of nutraceutical-grade zinc-carnosine within conventional antibiotic protocols
open_problems: Efficacy when added to bismuth quadruple or levofloxacin-based salvage regimens
Mechanism by which polaprezinc enhances eradication (antimicrobial zinc vs. mucosal-barrier improvement vs. immune modulation)
Generalisability to Western populations with different H. pylori strain distributions
